question: Even when no-decompression limits are strictly adhered to, the following factors can cause some divers to be more susceptible to decompression sickness:

options.c: Body weight
options.d: Dehydration

explanation: Fatigue, body weight, and dehydration are all predisposing factors for DCS even when staying within NDL.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: Signs and symptoms of lung overexpansion injuries include:

options.c: Euphoria
options.d: Breathing difficulties and chest pain

explanation: Breathing difficulty and chest pain are classic signs of lung overexpansion injury (e.g., AGE/pneumothorax).
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: When you begin to tire, lack the ability to make decisions, or lend support, it is time to:

options.c: Inflate your BC
options.d: Get Help

explanation: Recognize your limits and call for additional help — never compromise your own safety.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: Which level of stress is considered to be healthy?

options.a: A consistent high level of Stress.
options.b: A consistent low level of Stress, because it keeps us alert and warns us of danger.
options.c: A consistent medium level of Stress outside the water.
options.d: No Level of Stress.

explanation: A low level of stress keeps us alert and acts as an early warning system.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: The following are causes of psychological stress:

options.a: Lack of fitness, fatigue, constriction of movement and hypothermia
options.b: Lack of mental preparedness, mental control, and mental coping
options.c: Equipment loss, equipment failure and hyperthermia
options.d: Headache, fitness and a well maintained Total Diving System

explanation: Psychological stress stems from inadequate mental preparedness, control, and coping skills.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: The main reason people run out of breathing gas is:

options.c: Failure to make visual inspection every year.
options.d: Failure to monitor the pressure gauge or air integrated dive computer.

explanation: Out-of-air situations are almost always due to failing to monitor the SPG/air-integrated DC.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: The two most common dive maladies are:

options.c: Hyperhermia and CO toxicity
options.d: Shark bites and compression sickness

explanation: DCS and lung overexpansion injuries are the two most common pressure-related dive maladies.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: For mild and serious symptoms of DCS and Overexpansion injuries the appropriate first aid is:

options.c: Activate the emergency medical system
options.d: Watch vital signs

explanation: First aid for DCS/AGE includes 100% O2, activating EMS, and monitoring vitals — all of the above.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: In a buddy assist situation, which person do you have to take care first:

options.c: Yourself
options.d: The witnesses

explanation: Always secure your own safety first — you cannot help anyone if you become a second victim.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: Each and every safe dive starts with?

options.c: Entering the water with an giant stride.
options.d: Entering the water with a back-roll

explanation: Every safe dive begins with thorough pre-dive planning.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: Physical causes of stress include:

options.a: Headache, fitness and a well maintained Total Diving System
options.b: Lack of fitness, fatigue, constriction of movement and hypothermia
options.c: Poor judgment, buddy system failure and warm water
options.d: Equipment loss, equipment failure and hyperthermia

explanation: Physical stressors include poor fitness, fatigue, restricted movement, and hypothermia.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: Each diver will have slight variances in their dive plan that will affect their diving profile, especially during an entire week of diving. This is why:

options.a: You have to stay very close together if you use one computer with you buddy
options.b: Each diver must dive with their own computer
options.c: Each diver should drink more or less water
options.d: Each diver should use Nitrox 40 at the end of the week

explanation: Each diver's profile differs slightly, so each must use their own personal dive computer.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: If the victim is not breathing but has a pulse, your decision on your level of care will be based on:

options.a: Your distance from the shore or boat
options.c: The age of the victim
options.d: Your distance from the next group of divers

explanation: Distance to safety drives whether to provide rescue breaths in water or tow first then ventilate.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: Your personal preparedness to stay a step ahead of stress includes:

options.a: Wearing a isolation system, dive without a buddy and not deeper than allowed
options.b: Being healthy, having the proper skills, and using good quality and well-maintained equipment
options.c: Being healthy, having expensive equipment and using Nitrox 40 only
options.d: Being comfortable, having the number of dives needed and using rental equipment

explanation: Health, skills, and quality well-maintained gear are the foundations of stress prevention.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: Preparation in which area may be the life-saving difference in a panic or rescue situation?

options.c: Nitrox diving
options.d: Accident management

explanation: Accident management training ties together all rescue skills and is the life-saving differentiator.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: When you detect stress in your buddy, you need to?:

options.a: Ignore it, all certified divers can solve any stress situation by themselves
options.b: Immediately assess the source of that stress and deal with it appropriately
options.c: Keep your distance to not endanger youself if he/she starts panicking
options.d: Ascent and call for help

explanation: Identify the cause of stress immediately and address it before it escalates to panic.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: One of the best arguments in favor of always using the buddy system is:

options.a: In a stress situation there will almost always be at least two Dive Professionals which maintain control and are able to lend aid.
options.b: You will always have someone which can help you setting up your Total Diving System.
options.c: In a stress situation there will almost always be at least one diver who maintains control and is able to lend aid
options.d: You always can share your diving experiences after the dive.

explanation: The buddy system ensures at least one diver remains in control to render aid in a crisis.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: If you are separated from you dive buddy, you should:

options.c: Start searching until your breathing gas gets low, then surface
options.d: Surface after making two 360 degree visual sweeps (1 Minute)

explanation: Standard buddy-separation procedure: search no more than ~1 minute (two 360-degree sweeps), then surface.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: What is stress?

options.a: Always the result of pressure at depth and demands to the Total Diving System
options.b: The result of pressures or demands which outweigh a person's ability or capacity to respond to them
options.c: Stress is always the result of going diving
options.d: Nothing we should worry about

explanation: Stress occurs when demands exceed a person's capacity or ability to respond.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: All divers should get used to the idea that it's okay to make a no-go diving decision without fear of embarrassment, at which point?

options.a: Before setting up the Total Diving System
options.c: After entering the water but before descent
options.d: At any time

explanation: Any diver can call any dive at any time for any reason — no questions asked.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: Decisions about the best method of transportation should be based on the distance to the facility and:

options.c: The severity of the injury
options.d: The type of oxygen regulator used for first aid

explanation: Severity of injury combined with distance determines the appropriate evacuation method.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: Hypothermia is generally defined as:

options.a: The condition of having one's body temperature rising above normal.
options.b: The condition of having one's body temperature fall below normal
options.c: The condition of having one's body temperature normal
options.d: The condition of too much Oxygen at depth

explanation: Hypothermia = core body temperature falling below normal.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: Common skills for self-aid are:

options.b: Using alternate fin kicks and relieving leg cramps
options.c: All answers are correct
options.d: Switching to alternate air sources and regulator retrieval

explanation: All listed skills (buoyancy, emergency ascent, fin kicks, cramp relief, alternate air, reg retrieval) are self-aid skills.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: Factors which can impair movement and complicate the rescue process include:

options.c: Diving without wetsuit
options.d: Tight mask strap

explanation: Entanglement and entrapment are the principal factors that impair movement during a rescue.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

question: Before attempting to lift an unconscious diver into a boat or onto shore, you should:

options.c: Supply oxygen to the victim
options.d: Remove and ditch the victim's equipment

explanation: Ditch the victim's gear before extraction so the load is manageable and the airway/chest are accessible.
source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B
